The phrase "made to bow" and the elemental association with Stone suggest a megalithic site — a cairn, standing stones, or carved monolith — that has historically resisted oni or imperial authority and now must be subjugated, not destroyed. The reference to "proper law... spoken with sufficient force" implies the site is responsive to spoken or invoked authority of a particular kind, not raw violence.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Where is it? No location is given in the note; the party has not yet found a map or route to it
  • Who built it? Cairns in Tian Xia mark older powers — pre-imperial spirits, mountain shrines, or burial grounds
  • What does it currently "speak"? "Made to bow" implies it presently stands against something
  • In what order does Kurosawa intend to take it? The note lists the keys but does not commit to a strict sequence after the Hollow

docs.title: Session Nine (2026-05-07)
docs.text:
• Named for the first time in Kurosawa's note, recovered by Littlefinger from the hidden papers in the graveyard wall
  • Identified as the Stone key in Kurosawa's five-vein plan
  • Location and nature not yet known to the party

docs.title: The Dark Woods
docs.text: The trees grow dense and the light grows thin. At night, the woods earn their name.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Type Forest Region North of Willowshore, Chu Ye Danger Level Moderate (day) / Extreme (night)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Dark Woods lie immediately north of Willowshore, separated from the town by a clearing that contains the community graveyard and a few shrines. The forest is dense with deep green trees that grow thick and close together, blocking much of the light even during daytime. Within a few hundred yards of the tree line, visibility drops significantly.

The woods get their name from the dangers that emerge after dark. Willowshore residents universally avoid the forest at night, and the few who have ventured in after sunset have returned with stories of strange lights, unsettling sounds, and worse.

Despite this reputation, Willowshore has coexisted with the Dark Woods for many generations without serious incident—until now.

docs.title: The Mourndusk Willow
docs.text: Deep within the Dark Woods, but "not too deep" from Willowshore's northern edge, stands an extraordinary tree: a Mourndusk Willow.

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text:
• Black bark covering the trunk and roots
  • Black leaves that droop like a weeping willow
  • Deep, extensive roots that reach far underground and spread broadly
  • Unmistakable from the surrounding forest—entirely distinct from the regular willows that give the town its name

docs.title: Nature
docs.text: Mourndusk Willows are exceedingly rare "old earth trees" that cannot be propagated: - Taking a leaf or cutting produces nothing—they do not reproduce - They simply exist, remnants of ancient eons - Linked in folklore to dark magic, the occult, and cursed places - Said to be capable of poisoning the surrounding area and its animals - Despite this, Willowshore has existed near this one without apparent harm for generations

docs.title: Connections
docs.text:
• Magistrate Kurosawa has been visiting the Mourndusk Willow repeatedly since arriving in Willowshore. The Yeshou family has tracked his movements but does not know his purpose.
  • The glen where the tree stands is connected to a tragedy in the Yeshou family's past—linked to "the song that no one wants to finish."
  • Da Baishan's spirit lore found no connection to the spirit realm, despite the tree's occult associations.

docs.title: The Willowshore Graveyard
docs.text: At the edge of the Dark Woods, between the town and the tree line, lies Willowshore's community graveyard.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Family plots arranged in sections throughout the grounds
  • Small shrines in eastern architectural style (not western-style mausoleums)
  • Cricket cages — Small cages containing live crickets, placed at intervals throughout the graveyard. Their song is a traditional ward against restless spirits and undead. Standing near a cricket cage provides:
    • +1 to AC against undead
    • +1 on saving throws against fear and spirit effects
  • A grave tender maintains the grounds—a young woman in her 30s who was terrified when undead rose during Session Two

docs.title: The Undead Rising
docs.text:
During Session Two, the party encountered undead emerging from the graveyard for the first time in living memory: - A ghoul feeding on a recently exhumed body (someone who died within the past month) - A zombie shambling from between the graves - An undead cat lurking behind a tombstone

The grave tender and Donkey both confirmed this is unprecedented. Something is disrupting the natural cycle of life and death in Willowshore.

docs.title: The Ley Line Convergence
docs.text:
Mido revealed that Willowshore sits at a convergence of ley lines—arcane geomancy paths deep in the earth. Just as five rivers join on the surface, these invisible lines of magical energy cross beneath the village, creating a focal point that can be tapped to amplify magical power.

The Mourndusk Willow, with its ancient roots reaching deep into the earth, acts as a "lightning rod" for rituals that tap into these ley lines. This convergence is likely the true reason Magistrate Kurosawa was assigned to Willowshore—not just to oversee a political transition, but to exploit this magical nexus.

docs.title: The Summoning Ritual
docs.text: During Session Three, the party discovered evidence of a dark summoning ritual performed at the base of the Mourndusk Willow:

docs.title: Ritual Components Found
docs.text:
• A ceremonial steel dagger — Curved blade, ornate leather-wrapped handle, inscribed in Toanin (oni infernal language): "Let ancient law be sealed in the hand that guides Willowshore"
  • A clay bowl — Unglazed earthenware containing crushed moth wings (bodies removed) and traces of blood
  • A smooth depression — Between two roots at the tree's base, approximately two feet deep; the earth unnaturally smooth

docs.title: The Yellow Lichen Blight
docs.text: The ritual produced a lichen blight as a byproduct—a pale yellow, mossy growth that erupted from the east side of the tree in a blast pattern. Ginkgo (Nature 25) identified it as an arcane "bruise"—an after-effect of the energy released during the ritual, not a natural growth. The blight: - Was spreading to the tree's bark, roots, and nearby ground - Infected local wildlife (a bear and squirrels), giving them yellow eyes and aggressive behavior - Released sulfurous spores when disturbed (requiring Fortitude saves) - Could be neutralized by Ginkgo's fungal salve, turning it gray and causing it to flake off - The party fully cleansed the tree and surrounding area of the blight

docs.title: Identification
docs.text:
• Da Baishan (Occultism 24) identified the ritual as a dark sacrificial substitution—a communion or summoning that was at least partially successful
  • Mido identified the entity being summoned as the "Mother of a Thousand Wings"—an ancient demon of pestilence

docs.title: Session Two (2026-02-05)
docs.text:
• The Dark Woods and Mourndusk Willow described by Mido and identified by Ginkgo
  • Kurosawa's secret visits to the Mourndusk Willow revealed
  • The graveyard became the site of the party's first combat encounter
  • Undead rising identified as an unprecedented event for Willowshore
  • The party headed into the Dark Woods at mid-afternoon with ~6 hours of daylight remaining

docs.title: Session Three (2026-02-12)
docs.text:
• The party entered the Dark Woods and immediately noticed the forest felt different—colder, heavier, with an unnatural presence watching them
  • Reached the Mourndusk Willow clearing; discovered the yellow lichen blight and summoning ritual components
  • Fought blight-infected wildlife: a bear and two squirrels with yellow lichen and glowing yellow eyes
  • Ginkgo crafted a healing salve from his own body that neutralized the lichen; the party created a salve bomb that cured both squirrels
  • Da Baishan investigated the ritual objects; Ginkgo communed with the tree (it was "sad and battered")
  • The party spent the remaining daylight cleansing the tree and surrounding area of all blight
  • Mido later identified the ritual as a summoning of the Mother of a Thousand Wings and explained the ley line convergence beneath Willowshore

docs.title: The Gosembiki Ruins
docs.text: Five Pointed Star — where the ley lines converge and the land remembers what it was told to forget.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name Gosembiki (五芒星) — the Five Pointed Star Type Ancient ruins Location ~10–15 miles east of Willowshore, in the grassland plains along the river Status Abandoned; avoided by locals; recently investigated by Magistrate Kurosawa

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Gosembiki ruins are a cluster of dilapidated buildings visible from several miles away, jutting from the open grasslands east of Willowshore. The site consists of multiple structures—not a single building—suggesting it was once a settlement, temple complex, or ritual site of some significance.

Few locals visit or speak of it. Mido described it as "broken down" with "nothing really of value"—a place people avoid for reasons they cannot fully articulate. The name Gosembiki (五芒星, Five Pointed Star) was given by Magistrate Kurosawa in the Chthonic tongue and translated by Cheng Yesho.

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
The ruins are believed to be a convergence point—arcane, chthonic, possibly connected to the five ley lines that run beneath Willowshore and the surrounding region. A map and device diagram found among Kurosawa's papers marks this location, and the device sketch matches the apparatus Yong was commissioned to build.

Cassian Voss, the deceased researcher from Absalom, had planned to head east following the ley lines along the rivers—a path that would have led him directly to these ruins.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
The journey from Willowshore takes several hours on foot, following the river east. The terrain transitions from forest to sparse woodland to open grassland. The ruins become visible from about three to four miles away—a dark silhouette against the plains.

Near the ruins, a bridge crosses the river. The surrounding area has scattered tree outcroppings along the riverbank but is otherwise open ground.

As one approaches, the land itself deteriorates. Green grass gives way to patchy, darkened undergrowth. The earth becomes damp and squishy. Trees have blackened, split bark weeping dark sap. Insects buzz with what sounds like pain. A sweet, decayed smell—cinnamon and death—hangs in the air. Those attuned to nature can feel the land is wounded by something unnatural.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: From above, the ruin is clearly a five-pointed star shape, though partially collapsed and overgrown with moss, vines, and grass. The walls are highly carved stone—precise, linear patterns cover every visible surface, too mathematical to be random and too precise to be ornamental. Their purpose is unknown.

docs.title: Entrance
docs.text: A hidden opening (~4 feet wide) behind a mossy vine curtain, protected by an alarm tripwire (a filament that triggers a bell, not a damage trap). Disarmed by the party.

docs.title: Entry Hall
docs.text: A 20x20 rectangular room with collapsed pillars, empty pedestals, and overgrown vines. Two stone guardian constructs (dog-shaped, similar to the fox alarm at Kurosawa's estate) were stationed here—recently placed, not original to the ruin. Both destroyed by the party.

docs.title: Ritual Chamber
docs.text: A room containing a circular alabaster dial in the floor—white stone, distinct from the surrounding gray rock. The dial bears the symbol for "Mother"—connecting it to the Mother of a Thousand Wings. The dial was broken and scattered for ages, but someone recently reassembled the pieces like a puzzle. The party removed one piece to prevent full reassembly.

docs.title: Subterranean Chamber
docs.text: Accessed via a spiral staircase from a vine-choked corridor. The air grows dense and oppressive descending. A 30x30 stone chamber with geomantic patterns on walls and floor. At its heart: the device.

docs.title: The Device
docs.text:
A metallic flower-petal mechanism suspended inside a slowly turning gear track at the center of the subterranean chamber. The petals have carved fins that redirect energy as the mechanism rotates counterclockwise—very slowly, glacially. The device matches the diagram from Kurosawa's stolen map and the apparatus Yong was commissioned to build.

Function: The device warps the ley line beneath the ruins, redirecting its natural flow several degrees counterclockwise. This redirection is the source of the geomantic sickening that radiates outward—corrupting wolves, possessing leshies, blackening trees, and wounding the land. The school of magic is alteration/transformation, not necromancy.

Current status: Frozen in place by Donkey's Tangle Vine spell. Vines near the device's center burned away from the energy, but the outer bindings hold. A spectral guardian that defended the device has been destroyed.

docs.title: Session Five (2026-03-12)
docs.text:
• Mido revealed the existence of the Gosembiki—haunted ruins east of Willowshore that Kurosawa has named and recently traveled to
  • A stolen map from Kurosawa's desk marks the location with a device diagram

docs.title: Session Six (2026-03-19)
docs.text:
• The party trekked east along the river toward the ruins
  • Spotted the ruins from several miles away: a series of dilapidated buildings in the grasslands
  • Ambushed by an infected werewolf about a mile from the ruins—later revealed to be one of Mido's missing guards, riddled with cancerous pustules
  • The party camped approximately one mile from the ruins at dusk, planning to enter after resting

docs.title: Session Seven (2026-04-02)
docs.text:
• Party entered the ruins after defeating two corrupted wolves outside
  • Littlefinger found the hidden entrance and disarmed the alarm tripwire
  • Two stone guardian constructs (dog-shaped) animated and attacked; destroyed by the party
  • Discovered the "Mother" dial in the ritual chamber—recently reassembled by an unknown person; Donkey removed a piece
  • Two corrupted leshies (zombie-like, still conscious beneath the corruption) appeared and were put to Sleep by Donkey
  • Descended to the subterranean chamber and discovered the device—Yong's apparatus, actively warping the ley line
  • A spectral undead guardian attacked the party; destroyed by Boone after Donkey vine-wrapped the device to stop it
  • The party remains in the chamber, wounded but alive, with the device frozen

docs.title: Session Eight (2026-04-22)
docs.text:
• Da Baishan pulled the device free from its gear cradle after Littlefinger cleared it of traps (natural 20 Perception)
  • Catastrophic release: the device's slow ley-line warping had actually been winding potential energy like a spring; removing it unleashed all of it at once
  • Blinding light; birds in the surrounding sky exploded from heat venting; roof collapsed; party took bludgeoning damage (Da Baishan dropped to 0 and went dying; Donkey also went unconscious)
  • A chasm tore out of the ruin and arced across the countryside toward Willowshore—parallel to the river, still venting sulfurous steam and growing
  • Time froze in the collapsing chamber. Vujravati, a golden dragon, manifested, named itself as the voice of the shared dream, and gave each PC a personal mark/gift against "the falling darkness"
  • After time resumed, Donkey dug through the rubble and recovered four leshy bodies; Ginkgo performed a burial rite
  • Donkey also recovered an unblemished longsword in a sheath buried in the rubble: Judgement's Edge (see below), once wielded by the legendary oni Shen Takashi
  • The ruin is now functionally destroyed—a cratered collapse. The subterranean chamber is gone.

docs.title: Judgement's Edge
docs.text: A magical longsword found buried in the rubble, entirely unblemished despite the collapse. +1 magical longsword with a Rune of Striking. Once wielded by Shen Takashi, the Silent Duelist—a legendary oni ronin who broke from the oni leadership and dispensed his own justice across Chu Ye. Shen has not been seen in roughly twenty years; how his sword came to be in this ruin is unknown. Currently carried by Da Baishan (confiscated at the end of Session 8).

docs.title: The Hollow of Seven Cedars
docs.text: The old lovers' grove. A pool. Seven trees. A spirit that eats what it is given.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Name The Hollow of Seven Cedars (formerly Lovers' Grove) Type Ancient ritual site / sacred grove Location A few days' travel northwest of the Palatine Eye Vault; roughly west of Willowshore, across the river Status CORRUPTED — but salvageable. The Warden is dead, the Grandmother has spoken, and the party holds the stolen Root Correction ritual that can unbind her. Two seals lie on the grove — Kurosawa's, and an older unknown hand's — and 2–3 hostile presences still linger

Session Eleven — the key was already turned

The party reached the Hollow expecting to prevent the Root rite. They were too late. Kurosawa performed the grafting months ago — the cedars are already bleeding, the basin already inverted, the corruption already reaching toward Willowshore. What the party did here was not prevention but triage, and the cost of stopping it appears to be the death of the grove itself.

Session Twelve — a door out of the death sentence

The verdict was overturned. With the Warden dead and the basin cracked open, the Grandmother spoke to Ginkgo: the grove need not die if Kurosawa's binding rite — the Root Correction, the Second Key — is performed in reverse, by voice and by action. The party stole the only copy of the ritual from his estate that same night. The catch: Kurosawa's own notes reveal a second, older seal on the grove that his diagram "alone did not close" — and reversing his rite alone may not be enough.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Hollow of Seven Cedars is an ancient and rarely visited place west of Willowshore. Seven enormous cedars — redwood-thick, eons old — stand in a near-perfect ring, their high branches woven into a closed canopy. At the heart of the ring is an almost perfectly round stone basin, like an oversized bird-bath, ringed by ivory-white alabaster stones. In older days, when more people knew the road, it was called simply Lovers' Grove.

The site is held to be the home of a dream spirit — sometimes called the Dream Eater, or the Grandmother. Tradition holds that the spirit's gender is unknown and unimportant; it accepts what is given to it and asks nothing else.

docs.title: As the party found it (Session Eleven)
docs.text:
The grove was wrong long before the party reached the ring. On the approach trail they found a cut red ribbon and a broken child's toy — grave-tokens that belonged at the grove, somehow strewn away from it. The forest itself was healthy (no wounded-land sickness like the Dark Woods), but Ginkgo felt a dissociation in the air, and an Augury confirmed something was deeply off.

Inside the ring:

  • The white alabaster stones were blackened and marbled, marred where they had been unblemished
  • The central pool was inky, light-swallowing black — no reflection, no shimmer
  • Heavy copper nails — railroad-tie thick, sledge-driven — were hammered into every cedar, bark-side facing the basin, wounding the trees. Black, inky sap bled from each wound, exactly like the corruption at the Mourndusk Willow
  • On the third cedar, ~20 inches off the ground, frantic knife-scratches had gouged out a carved name, leaving only the letters ·S·S·I·N·V·O·S·S — Cassian Voss. In the gouges: a fine khaki/mustard powder, identified as finely-ground moth-wing carapace — the same signature as the moths that killed the lanterns at the mayor's inauguration and the wings found at the willow

docs.title: The Grandmother / Dream Eater
docs.text:
The grove's spirit went by several names — most often the Grandmother or the Dream Eater. The legend Littlefinger and Donkey recalled (Society 23) holds that if she answered your plea, she would eat the dark memory you brought her — the grief, the guilt, the thing you could not carry — and let you keep the good. The eaten memory was said to poison her in your place; that was the bargain. Pilgrims always brought a memento (a ribbon, a soldier's medal, a dead child's toy) as the anchor of the thing to be removed.

The function was fundamentally subtractive and protective: she took the worst of what people remembered so the rest could heal. Kurosawa's corruption inverts exactly this — see below.

docs.title: The Old Practice
docs.text:
Pilgrims came to the Hollow to cast off the dreams and desires they wished to be free of, and to keep the ones they cherished.

The rite, as Mido recalls it:

  1. Bring a trinket that anchors the unwanted dream — a child's toy, a soldier's badge, a lover's token, an emblem of war
  2. Place the trinket inside one of the seven cedars (the trees have been worn smooth by hands and grief)
  3. Speak the dream or memory into the pool at the heart of the grove
  4. Walk away changed — relieved of the harshest edge of the thing remembered, but not of the memory itself

People came here for many reasons:

  • A spouse with a wandering eye, asking for the desire to be removed
  • A grieving parent, asking to keep love for a dead child but not the daily, gnawing pain
  • Veterans of old wars, depositing badges and dog-tags they could no longer carry
  • Lovers who wished to forget another lover, or to remember only the good of a parted one

The place has long been considered private and sacred — even those who do not believe in spirits avert their eyes when passing nearby.

docs.title: Significance to Kurosawa's Plan
docs.text:
Kurosawa's recovered note (the Note of Five Keys) names the Hollow as the second of his five ley-line targets. He labels it the Root key:

"The old lovers' grove, now called the Hollow of Seven Cedars, must be opened at the root next. Memory can be cut, grafted, and made to grow in a new direction."

Mido interprets this as Kurosawa intending to invert the spirit's old function — instead of helping people cast off unwanted memory, the Dream Eater would be made to prune and graft memory in the townspeople of Willowshore. What the survivors would forget — and what they would remember instead — would be Kurosawa's choice.

"Will the surviving people forget their loved ones? Will they remember only the dark thoughts? I'm not exactly sure what he wants with it, but it seems very important to his next phase." — Mido

If the legends are true and the spirit still exists, it is presumably still bound to the cedars and the pool by the same agreement that held it there for centuries.

docs.title: Kurosawa's Rite (the vision in the pool)
docs.text:
When Da Baishan touched the black water — drawn by the eye-mark warming on the back of his hand — the basin showed him, fisheye-from-below, what was done here. It was the first time the party has watched Kurosawa perform a key ritual:

  • Kurosawa knelt alone at the basin under recent moonlight, hands black with wet soil, a sledge-mallet and a pouch of long copper nails at his side
  • He held a severed, blackened root about a foot and a half long, which squirmed hungrily toward the water as he chanted
  • He spoke an incantation — the line he mumbled, that the vision carried up to the party:

"Memory is not sacred. It is only precedent. And precedent can be overturned."

  • He pressed the root into the water; its inky coating bled off into the pool; it grafted itself onto one of the great cedar roots in the basin floor and the blackness streaked outward through all seven interconnected root systems

The vision then cut to Willowshore to show the purpose: husbands remembering only disappointment, children remembering only fear, neighbors forgetting debts of kindness. Liwen looking at the party without recognition. Radiant Willow lowering a hand she had once raised for them — no longer able to remember why she trusted them. Yong keeping the memory of his rescued son, but fixating instead on the belief that the party caused the danger in the first place. The grove's old gift — eat the dark, keep the good — turned exactly inside out, the corruption flowing from the Seven Cedars into the town.

This is the Root key in operation: not a weapon, but a machine for poisoning a town's memory of itself.

docs.title: The Verdict: The Trees Must Die
docs.text:
The vision left Da Baishan with one final, flat truth, and Ginkgo's own consultation with the mycelium (Nature/ecology 21) confirmed it:

  • The poison is in all seven cedars' interconnected roots, not just the grafted one
  • Pulling the nails is not enough. Draining the basin is not enough. Tearing out the master graft is not enough
  • There is no known cure and no extraction — this is arcane/occult corruption, not a natural blight or poison that can be salved
  • The only paths even hypothesized: knowing the originating ritual (to reverse it), or going back before it was implemented
  • To stop the corruption, the grove itself — all seven ancient cedars — must die. Killing the grove also presumably ends the Dream Eater's gift forever

Ginkgo, a leshy, found this almost impossible to accept ("these are old trees, older than all of us") — and then jumped into the pool anyway to try.

docs.title: The Pool's Second Vision (Ginkgo)
docs.text:
When Ginkgo leapt in, the basin showed him a different scene — a test rather than a record: two childhood friends laughing on a bench; one called to war and one not; they swear they will never be enemies. Seasons blur; a battlefield; two soldiers duel; the victor lifts the slain one's helmet and it is the friend who went to war. The water asked:

"Who do you choose? Even when there is no blood law or prophet bind — who would you save?"

Ginkgo answered: his childhood friend from the village he left behind. The vision honored the answer, and the water drained into the earth, leaving him at the bottom of the dry basin beside the throbbing, heartbeat-alive master graft.

docs.title: The Warden of the Grove
docs.text:
The instant Boone ripped the master graft free (Athletics 25) — black ichor splattering, the root writhing for fifteen seconds before going limp and ashen — the trees outside the ring swayed and a horned creature tore out of the treeline, howling at the sky. Da Baishan's Occultism identified it: a Warden of the Grove, the grove's protector, and it looked angry.

In Session Twelve the party fought it — trying and failing to subdue it — and killed it. It proved to be a puppet: Ginkgo's mid-combat ecology read found two, maybe three opposed, unnatural presences crowding the grove and pulling on the creature, "like a city of chattering voices." Those presences survived its death and still linger in the Hollow.

docs.title: The Grandmother Unbound — Almost (Session Twelve)
docs.text:
After the Warden fell, the drained basin cracked and vented — an exhale of air and mist — revealing through the fissures the vast root-lattice interconnecting all seven cedars. From beneath it, the Grandmother spoke to Ginkgo (and only Ginkgo — as the pool's speaker):

  • She has been bound nearly a year, inverted, forced to eat Willowshore's good memories down the ley line
  • The grove does not have to die. Reverse the binding rite — the Root Correction, the Second Key, Kurosawa's custom geomantic ritual — and she can "open my mouth again... and eat the darkness," stopping any further corruption
  • The reversal requires the ritual's steps performed in reverse order, by voice and by actions — "give back the name and undo the seals." The originator is not required
  • What's already lost stays lost: "what went downhill must remain downhill." Willowshore's eaten memories cannot be restored

The party stole the ritual — the only copy, in Kurosawa's own hand — from his estate chambers that night, along with five spare copper nails and his lockbox note.

docs.title: The Two Seals
docs.text:
The stolen papers, analyzed at camp against everything the party knows, revealed that the Hollow carries two distinct seals from two different hands:

Seal One — the Root Correction Seal Two — the Erasure Hand Kurosawa — confirmed by his own papers Unknown — explicitly not Kurosawa; he is investigating it himself Character Orderly geomantic lines, diagrams, transformation Frantic knife-work — "haste, emotion, emotional removal" Physical trace The copper nails, the grafted root, the inverted pool The scratched-out Cassian Voss name, packed with moth-wing powder Age Recent — within months of the party's arrival Older — 6 months to a year; before Kurosawa's nails Effect Inverted the Grandmother's gift; aimed it at Willowshore "Shut the mouth" of the grove — an undoing that Kurosawa found already in place and built on top of

Kurosawa's note is blunt about the dependency: "My nails hold on top of another hand's work. Useful. But it means my diagram alone will not open what it alone did not close." The party's open worry is the mirror image: whether reversing his ritual alone can free the Grandmother from a binding that partially predates him. Her "nearly a year" timeline matches the erasure, not the nails.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text: Roughly 10 miles west of Willowshore. The trail passes through scattered woods that thicken as the road descends toward the grove. The cedars are visible from a distance — seven dark spires above the canopy, taller than the surrounding trees, set in a near-perfect ring.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Will the reversal work? The party holds the Root Correction and the Grandmother's instructions — but Kurosawa's own note warns his diagram sits on top of a seal it did not create. Does the reversal need to address both seals?
  • What is "the name" that must be given back? Voss's scratched-out name is the obvious candidate, but unconfirmed
  • What are the lingering presences? Two or three, mutually opposed, still in the grove after the Warden's death. Will they resist a ritual performed in their midst — and will the grove raise another Warden?
  • Whose is the second seal? Voss's own work, the work of whatever killed him, or "some ancient power"? Kurosawa himself doesn't know
  • What killed Cassian Voss here, and whose handwriting did Littlefinger and Donkey each half-recognize?

docs.title: Session Nine (2026-05-07)
docs.text:
• Named for the first time in Kurosawa's note, recovered by Littlefinger from the graveyard wall crevice
  • Mido explained its history and traditional use — the Dream Eater, the seven cedars, the pool, the trinkets
  • Identified by Mido as Kurosawa's likely next target (the Root key)
  • The party deferred travel to the Hollow in favor of going south first, to the Order of the Palatine Eye (~50 miles away) for occult counsel before facing the Dream Eater

docs.title: Session Eleven (2026-06-04)
docs.text:
• The party arrived from the southwest to find the grove already corrupted — Kurosawa's Root rite was complete, not pending
  • Found the approach littered with displaced grave-tokens; the alabaster ring stones blackened; copper nails bleeding inky sap from every cedar; the basin gone light-swallowing black
  • On the third cedar, Cassian Voss's name had been carved and violently scratched out, packed with ground moth-wing powder; the carving's faint magic dated 6 months to a year old — months before Kurosawa's nails
  • Ginkgo's Augury on pulling the nails returned "no positive outcome"; the party left the nails in place
  • Da Baishan touched the pool and witnessed Kurosawa performing the grafting rite, including the incantation "Memory is not sacred. It is only precedent. And precedent can be overturned," and the projected fate of Willowshore's memory
  • The pool's final truth + Ginkgo's mycelium check confirmed the trees must die — no cure, no extraction
  • Ginkgo jumped into the pool, answered its "who would you save?" test (his childhood friend), and drained the basin
  • Boone tore out the master graft root (Athletics 25) — and a Warden of the Grove erupted from the treeline, howling. Session ended on its arrival.

docs.title: Session Twelve (2026-07-16)
docs.text:
• The party fought and killed the Warden after failed attempts to subdue and communicate with it; fire proved its weakness, blades nearly useless
  • Ginkgo's mid-combat reads revealed the Warden was puppeted by 2–3 opposed, unnatural presences crowding the grove — presences that remained after its death
  • The basin cracked and vented, exposing the interlinked root system; the Grandmother spoke to Ginkgo for the first time — see The Grandmother Unbound — Almost above
  • The path forward was set: steal and reverse the Root Correction. The party marched to Willowshore, robbed Kurosawa's chambers, and returned west with the ritual, five spare nails, and the lockbox note revealing the two seals

docs.title: Kirahata
docs.text: The pleasure city on the southern coast—where gold flows as freely as the wine.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Type City Region Southern coast of Chu Ye Nickname The "Vegas" of Chu Ye Notable Resident Mayor Masru (formerly)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Kirahata (also rendered as Purahata) is a large coastal city on the southern shore of Chu Ye, several days' journey from Willowshore. It is known as a hedonistic pleasure city—a center of entertainment, gambling, and nightlife that attracts the wealthy and famous from across the region.

Mayor Masru hails from Kirahata, where he ran several businesses, attempted a career as a sumo competitor (earning the nickname "Sumo of Smiles"), and frequented establishments like the Red Orchid—a gentleman's club. He mentioned that Meilin could "make herself and myself very wealthy" working there.

docs.title: Known Establishments
docs.text: Location Description The Red Orchid A gentleman's club; Masru referenced it when commenting on Meilin's appearance

docs.title: Session Six (2026-03-19)
docs.text:
• Mentioned by Mayor Masru as his hometown; he described it as a place of shows, singers, wealthy patrons, and entertainment
  • Radiant Willow repeated Masru's festival stories about his life in Kirahata
  • The party noted it would be several days' journey from Willowshore and may have fortune tellers or other specialists not available locally

docs.title: Palatine Eye Vault
docs.text: A cliff with no door. A puzzle of murals, scrolls, and a lantern that lies. A book that only its keeper can read.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name The Palatine Eye Vault (Hall of Divided Testimony) Type Sealed occult chamber of the Order of the Palatine Eye Location Cliffside in the mountains, ~40–50 miles south of Willowshore; ~15 miles south of Glutton's Bridge Status Opened by Da Baishan in Session Ten; sealed itself behind the party as they left

docs.title: Approach: Glutton's Bridge
docs.text:
The path south winds through cool mountain forest — pine, deciduous green, the last of the cherry-blossom trees losing petals in the wind. The trail descends into a small ravine spanned by a 10–12 foot stone footbridge, with no water below. The bridge is old, mortared stone, and someone built it long enough ago that the design is no longer in current use.

A glutton ogre — roughly 9 feet tall, two-legged, ambush-disciplined enough to blend against the rocks — has claimed the southern end of the bridge as a hunting ground. The party watched it run down a passing elk by hand. They did not engage; they distracted it with a bamboo trebuchet flinging a shuriken-loaded cured ham, and crossed while it was busy.

docs.title: The Cliffside Door
docs.text:
About 15 miles south of the bridge, after a couple of switchbacks at altitude, the path runs straight into a sheer rock cliff. There is no door, no carving, no gate. Only Da Baishan — carrying Vujravati's location-mark — feels the cliff's pull, like being inside the field of a magnet.

The door opens for a single sequence:

  1. The hand that doubts knocks. Donkey's reasoning — "my hand doubts that knocking will work, so it is the doubting hand" — got him through the threshold of the riddle and to a knock; but the cliff was solid stone for him.
  2. Da Baishan's knock answered. Two raps on the same stone produced a different sound — metallic, deeper than rock — and a closed eye traced itself in the cliff face. The same eye drew itself, simultaneously, in henna-style ink on the back of Da Baishan's hand.
  3. The eye opens. When the figure completed, the cliff opened along the line of the eye, revealing a 30-step stone staircase descending ~20 feet into a sealed circular chamber.

The eye on Da Baishan's hand opened with the door, and stayed open as long as the door stood open. Da Baishan recalled, like a struck bell, that Cassian Voss had this same mark on the back of his hand.

docs.title: The Hall of Divided Testimony
docs.text:
The first chamber is a 20-foot circular room with a panoramic mural of pen-and-ink scenes painted continuously around the curved wall. The far side is a stone door with four bronze dials and an inscription:

"Lies do not bury truth. They show where truth was wounded."

The dials are labeled Moon, Willow, Bridge, and Flame. A jade dragon-coil ring at the center of the dial array locks the answer when turned.

In the ceiling: a black metal lantern with a steady blue flame that gives off no heat. On the floor: four short scrolls, like fortune-cookie strips, named for elements of the puzzle.

docs.title: The trick
docs.text:
Each of the four scrolls reads as a literal hint to the dial it shares a theme with. Each scroll is also lying. The mural shows where each lie is wounded.

The lantern is the key — but only when the scrolls are read aloud to it. When read into the lantern's light, the cast shadows lengthen across the floor, reach the mural, and reshape the painted scene to show the truth that the spoken lie revealed:

Scroll read aloud Mural answers Dial set to Pale Witness (Moon) Moon shifts from the first arrival tower to the opposite tower (the one nearest the sealed door) Right tower Weeping Root (Willow) A trail of black leaves climbs from the courtyard, past the grave marker, up to the shrine roof; the willow's branches hang from the roof like prayer cords Shrine roof Loyal Crossing (Bridge) The left and right arches walk away as painted soldiers; only the center arch remains One arch Devout Flame (Flame) The kneeling monk stands, raises his left hand palm-out, swears a vow with a flame above his head; the eye appears on the back of that left hand as he lowers it Monk's left hand

The Order's solution is therefore: Right tower / Shrine roof / One arch / Monk's left hand. The dragon-ring locked, the door ground 10 feet back into the wall, and a 30-foot ink-dark hallway formed from the ceiling pouring down — revealing the inner sanctum.

"Lies do not bury truth. They show where truth was wounded."

docs.title: The Sanctum and the Stone Witness
docs.text:
The inner chamber is another circular room, ~20 ft across. In the center: a kneeling stone figure, ~12 feet tall when standing, with no mouth and a single closed eye in its face. It is not standing guard — it is in the posture of a witness called and waiting.

Above and behind the figure, in the same script as the outer hall:

"The eye does not open for strength. It opens for the hand that accepts being seen."

When Boone approached with his eye-marked hand raised (the same mark Da Baishan had taken from the cliff knock), the statue stood, opened its single eye, raised the same hand, and walked, robotic, to the wall — placing its palm under the inscription. A small alcove opened beneath, holding a lectern and a single book.

The statue did not move again. It is still standing inside the sealed cliffside, in the open posture, watching the door.

docs.title: The Book of Divided Testimony
docs.text:
A leather-bound mage-style tome, locked by a strap and a mechanical clasp with no keyhole. The cover reads, The Book of Divided Testimony.

To the rest of the party, the book is blank. Every page Boone turned was empty paper. To Boone alone the pages held words.

The eye-tattoo on the back of his hand grew warm against the lock; the latch released to him directly. The first page is the Order's catechism:

"Truth does not rescue. Truth is responsibility."

The book is offered as a class-style dedication into the Order — Boone may take the Palatine Detective archetype after reading it. Beyond the dedication pages, the book contains fragments of testimony from past members of the Order, available as the bearer earns them. Boone rolled twice on a 1d8 random table; two of eight possible truths revealed themselves to him on first reading. Six remain.

docs.title: Truth I — Mido's Half-Truth
docs.text:
Beware the elder who lies by subtraction.

She does not hide the road because she serves the dark.

She hides the road because she knows what followed the last traveler home.

A tyrant believes law can replace loyalty.

A geomancer believes lines can replace roots.

This entry concerns Mido. It does not accuse her of treachery; it accuses her of omission. It identifies her secrecy not as collaboration but as fear of provoking the same outcome again — "what followed the last traveler home." The party does not yet know who that traveler was.

docs.title: Truth II — Kurosawa's Error
docs.text:
A tyrant believes law can replace loyalty.

A geomancer believes lines can replace roots.

Both are wrong until the roots are made to forget themselves.

This entry names Magistrate Kurosawa's error, and gives the party — for the first time — the theoretical objection to his ritual. Kurosawa's plan is conditional. The Mother of a Thousand Wings cannot be invoked through ley-line geomancy alone. The roots themselves must be made to forget what they are. That is exactly the function of the Hollow of Seven Cedars, the Root key, and exactly the role of its Dream Eater in his rite.

The mismatch between Kurosawa's theory and his actual plan is the seam in his confidence the Order means the party to find. If the Hollow's spirit refuses to forget, the entire Confluence fails.

docs.title: Sealing
docs.text:
The party left the sanctum carrying the book. The eye on the cliff face closed silently behind them. The cliff is now smooth stone again, with no visible seam. Only the eye-tattoo on the back of Boone's hand — and his copy of the Book of Divided Testimony — would let them back in.

Da Baishan chose to drop his cliff-mark tattoo on the way out, ceding the role of the Order's named bearer to Boone, who was the one the book had answered.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Why was Cassian Voss marked with the same eye? Was he Order? An associate? A failed candidate?
  • Are the other six truths in the book person-named? Place-named? Tied to the keys?
  • The lantern, the murals, the stone witness — none decayed. Are these actively maintained, or held in stasis?
  • The cliff sealed when the party left. Does it open for any other party member, ever, now that Da Baishan dropped the mark and Boone took the book?

docs.title: Session Ten (2026-05-14)
docs.text:
• The party crossed Glutton's Bridge by ham-trebuchet, then climbed several switchbacks to a hermit's landing where they met Cliché, who told them to "seek the cliff that has no door, knock with the hand that doubts"
  • Da Baishan reached the cliff in Vujravati's gift; Donkey misfired the riddle; Da Baishan's two-knock pattern was the answer, drew the eye, opened the door, and marked his hand
  • Inside, the four-dial puzzle was solved by reading the four scrolls aloud to the lantern — the cast shadows reshaped the mural to show the wounded truth, dial by dial: Right tower / Shrine roof / One arch / Monk's left hand
  • The inner chamber's stone witness rose at Boone's eye-marked approach, walked to the inscription, opened the alcove with the lectern
  • Boone took the Book of Divided Testimony; first page revealed "Truth does not rescue. Truth is responsibility."
  • Two of eight possible book-truths revealed themselves on first reading: Mido's Half-Truth and Kurosawa's Error
  • Boone elected the Palatine Detective dedication; Da Baishan dropped his eye-mark tattoo, ceding it to Boone
  • The party left; the cliff sealed behind them with no visible door

docs.title: The River's Lantern Spine
docs.text: "Water carries every name."

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name The River's Lantern Spine Type Submerged ritual site / drowned shrine line (presumed) Location Unknown — somewhere along a river course in the Willowshore region Status Named only in Kurosawa's note — the fourth of five ley-line "keys"

docs.title: What is Known
docs.text:
The River's Lantern Spine is named in Kurosawa's planning note as the Water key. The full passage:

"The drowned markers of the River's Lantern Spine must be turned with the current. Water carries every name."

The language strongly implies a series of submerged stone or wooden markers ("drowned markers") set in a riverbed in a line — a "spine" of lantern stones, perhaps once lit ceremonially, now swallowed by the river. "Turned with the current" suggests the markers must be physically realigned to flow with the water, not against it — reversing or redirecting whatever they were originally set to do.

The maxim "Water carries every name" echoes both Tian Xia funeral language (rivers carry the dead's names downstream to the sea) and Kurosawa's broader interest in renaming Willowshore — softening its history so that no name fixes in place except the one he chooses.

docs.title: Speculation
docs.text: Willowshore sits at a confluence of rivers, and Mido has long held that the five surface rivers mirror the five ley lines beneath the land. The River's Lantern Spine may run along one of those rivers — possibly even one in or very near Willowshore itself — though the exact location is unknown.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Which river? Yanzy and its tributaries each could host such a line
  • What did the markers originally do? Lights for spirits, way-stones for funerals, boundary markers between domains
  • Who tends them now? No one the party has yet met has spoken of them by name
  • Is "turned with the current" a mechanical action, a ritual one, or both?

docs.title: Session Nine (2026-05-07)
docs.text:
• Named for the first time in Kurosawa's note, recovered by Littlefinger from the hidden papers in the graveyard wall
  • Identified as the Water key in Kurosawa's five-vein plan
  • Location and nature not yet known to the party

docs.title: The Terrace of Whispering Clay
docs.text: "Clay receives the hand. Clay remembers the shape."

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name The Terrace of Whispering Clay Type Sealed ritual terrace / clay shrine (presumed) Location Unknown — somewhere in the Willowshore region Status Named only in Kurosawa's note — the fifth and final of five ley-line "keys"

docs.title: What is Known
docs.text:
The Terrace of Whispering Clay is named in Kurosawa's planning note as the Ash key — and explicitly as the last to be sealed. The full passage:

"The Terrace of Whispering Clay must be sealed last. Clay receives the hand. Clay remembers the shape."

The phrase "sealed last" sets this site apart from the others. The first four keys are forced into accord, opened, made to bow, and turned; only the Terrace is sealed. This, paired with the elemental association of Ash and the line "clay remembers the shape", suggests the Terrace is the finishing surface of the ritual — the place where the new shape of Willowshore is impressed and held, so that what has been transformed cannot relax back to its old form.

The "whispering" of the clay may be literal: a place where pressed shapes (handprints, carvings, footprints) speak. Such terraces are documented elsewhere in Tian Xia at sites associated with potter-gods, kiln spirits, and ancestor-marking rites.

docs.title: Speculation
docs.text: If Willowshore is the chrysalis Kurosawa describes — a city whose history is to be softened and whose loyalties are to be dissolved — then the Terrace of Whispering Clay is the mold that holds the new shape after every other key has done its work. Sealing the Terrace last would lock the transformation in place permanently, beyond reversal.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Where is it? Likely close to or inside Willowshore, given its role as the finishing site
  • What is currently impressed in the clay? Centuries of village handprints, carvings, ancestor-marks?
  • Can the seal be broken once placed? The note implies not, but does not say so
  • Does Kurosawa intend to seal it himself, or use a proxy?

docs.title: Session Nine (2026-05-07)
docs.text:
• Named for the first time in Kurosawa's note, recovered by Littlefinger from the hidden papers in the graveyard wall
  • Identified as the Ash key in Kurosawa's five-vein plan and the final site to be sealed
  • Location and nature not yet known to the party

docs.title: Willowshore
docs.text: A place of faded glory and stubborn heart.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Type Village Region Northern Chu Ye River Yanzy River Government Mayor Masru (oni); formerly de facto rule by local family

docs.title: Regional Map
docs.text: Willowshore sits at the confluence of rivers, surrounded by dense forest. A lake lies to the east.

docs.title: Town Map
docs.text: The village features a central manor, torii gate with bridge, scattered buildings, farmland, and a graveyard. Dense forest presses in from the south.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Nestled on a slow bend of the rain-swollen Yanzy River, the town of Willowshore is a place of faded glory and stubborn heart. Here at the edge of Chu Ye's wild, mysterious forest, old festivals are honored even as old wounds fester. Strangers' footsteps are watched, and secrets settle thick as mist along the water's edge.

The town sits at a junction of rivers—once a thriving logging and mining hub, now a skeleton of its former self. The closest shore leads north to the sea; the rivers join together into a large lake to the east.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Willowshore had a heyday long ago, but those days are past. The old industries collapsed, and the population dwindled. For generations, the village was too small and poor to warrant attention from Shogun Tsuneni's regime. In the oni's absence, a local family rose to become de facto rulers.

Now, after generations without an oni mayor, one is finally arriving—claiming to have "always been here." This has stirred unrest and reopened old divisions.

docs.title: The Revival of Friendship
docs.text:
A traditional rite observed at every change of power—whether a new store manager or a new mayor—where old grudges are publicly put to rest. It serves as an unofficial but very customary Transfer of Power.

The ruling family is refusing to participate in the ceremony for the new oni mayor. This defiance has created tension throughout the village.

docs.title: Festival of Returning Spirits
docs.text: An ancient festival held on the eve of autumn, meant to protect Willowshore from whatever haunts the dark woods beyond. The whole town gathers as lanterns flicker and drums thrum. Participants are chosen to lead a ceremonial play, walking in the shadows as the founders once did—acting out the fragile bargain that keeps the unknown at bay.

docs.title: The Rite of Open Reckoning
docs.text:
Not a Willowshore tradition but a Chu Ye imperial legal procedure that was invoked here by Magistrate Kurosawa during Session 8. It is an open court hearing: the accused are tried publicly in front of the whole town. Anyone holding public office may invoke the Rite. A judge—typically the highest local authority—hears the evidence, and the judge's ruling is final. The crowd does not vote; they witness.

Both prosecution and defense may call witnesses. The accused may speak in their own defense. The Rite is rarely invoked in modern practice, and carries weight precisely because of its severity.

In the party's case, the mayor served as judge. The verdict was guilty.

docs.title: The Forest Story
docs.text:
The oldest story villagers whisper about the forest shadows involves will-o-wisps that lead people astray into the woods.

Why no one speaks the ending:

The story's ending is never told aloud—not because it's forgotten, but because no one can agree on it, and speaking it starts fights. The disagreement runs deep, touching on old loyalties and resentments.

The truth, known to few: The ending embarrasses the ruling family. The heir to the family fortune was said to have been "lured away by forest spirits." In reality, he simply got drunk and wandered into the woods. His father—a teetotaler—spun the tale about spirits to save face.

This deception created factions. Some people secretly blame each other; some welcomed the oni as a chance to unseat the old rulers. One member of the ruling family (The Smiling One) whispers when they think no one is listening: "I know the real ending."

docs.title: Local Customs
docs.text:
• Averting eyes from oni: When oni enter town, lower classes must avert their gaze—only the ruling class may look upon them
  • "I've always been here": The official stance that oni have always been present, despite obvious evidence otherwise
  • Spirit death rituals: Local legend holds that death near a spirit requires special disposal of the body, lest it return or be controlled

docs.title: Notable Locations
docs.text: Location Description The Yeshou Estate Former manor of the ruling family; now occupied by Magistrate Kurosawa The Torii Gate Traditional gate at the bridge crossing The Graveyard Cemetery with family plots, shrines, and cricket cages near the Dark Woods edge The River Site of strange occurrences; where Ginkgo and Da Baishan witnessed a spirit killing Eternal Blaze Ironworks Blacksmith shop run by Yong, a young kitsune Silver Mist Lodge Once-grand inn on the riverside; where the ruling family is temporarily staying Dew Drop Petals Salon and spa run by Radiant Willow Cloud Paper Lodge Paper lantern shop; their fireworks were stored in the warehouse Seven Colored Songbird Theater Open-air theater north of the bridge; site of performances, ceremonies, and the Festival of Returning Spirits transfer of power Liwen's Flower Cart Tiny florist shop (barely more than a cart) run by Liwen Luda's Shop Seamstress and informal arcane supplier run by Luda; deals in rare plants, alchemy ingredients, and spell components The Luckless Cod Local tavern where workers drink after hours The Warehouse Dock storage for festival fireworks; partially destroyed by fire The Dark Woods Dense forest to the north; home of the Mourndusk Willow; dangerous at night

docs.title: Oni Regime
docs.text:
• Magistrate Kurosawa — Blue-skinned oni magistrate enforcing martial law
  • Mayor Masru — The affable blue-skinned oni mayor, now in residence
  • Four red-skinned oni guards in samurai armor

docs.title: Residents
docs.text:
• Mido — Matriarch of the Yeshou family
  • The Smiling One — Ruling family member who "knows the real ending"
  • Donkey — Ancient elf wizard; village witch
  • Yong — Kitsune blacksmith at Eternal Blaze Ironworks
  • Hong — Yong's son; hired to set the warehouse fire
  • Radiant Willow — Flower leshy beautician at Dew Drop Petals

docs.title: Visitors
docs.text:
• Da Baishan — Orc investigator drawn by suspicion of the oni
  • Boone — Dwarf fighter from distant lands
  • Ginkgo — Fungus leshy cleric gathering information
  • Littlefinger — Halfling rogue with ties to the ruling family
  • Cassian Voss — (deceased) Researcher from Absalom who studied "old gods"; now an untethered spirit

docs.title: Session Zero (2026-01-16)
docs.text:
• Village established as the campaign's starting location
  • World-building created the Revival of Friendship tradition
  • The forest story and its forbidden ending established
  • "The Smiling One" created as a mystery NPC
  • The torii gate, bridge, and river featured in character bonds

docs.title: Session One (2026-01-30)
docs.text:
• Magistrate Kurosawa introduced as the advance guard for the mayor
  • The ruling family displaced from their estate to Silver Mist Lodge
  • A warehouse fire destroyed many festival preparations; children rescued by the party
  • Multiple new locations discovered and visited by the party

docs.title: Session Two (2026-02-05)
docs.text:
• Hong revealed as the arsonist, hired by the Yeshou family
  • Mido introduced as the family matriarch; admitted to the sabotage and canceled further plans
  • Kurosawa's secret visits to the Mourndusk Willow in the Dark Woods revealed
  • Undead rose in the graveyard for the first time in living memory
  • The party fought a ghoul, a zombie, and an undead cat at the cemetery
  • A spirit connected to Ginkgo and Da Baishan's secret appeared during and after combat
  • Yong planning to leave Willowshore; "sees dark skies ahead"

docs.title: Session Three (2026-02-12)
docs.text:
• The party explored the Dark Woods and discovered a dark summoning ritual at the Mourndusk Willow
  • The river spirit identified as Cassian Voss, a foreign researcher from Absalom
  • Fought blight-infected wildlife at the Mourndusk Willow; Ginkgo created a salve to cleanse the lichen blight
  • Mido identified the ritual as a summoning of the "Mother of a Thousand Wings"—an ancient demon of pestilence
  • Willowshore revealed to sit at a convergence of ley lines—arcane geomancy paths that mirror the five rivers on the surface
  • Mido disclosed secret entrances into the Yeshou estate (now occupied by the magistrate)
  • Yong delivered runed weapons to the party; confirmed plans to leave for a coastal town after the festival
  • Hong reported no further sabotage plots among the town's children
  • The Festival of Returning Spirits is set for the next evening

docs.title: Session Four (2026-02-26)
docs.text:
• The Festival of Returning Spirits took place: procession, tea transfer ceremony at the Seven Colored Songbird Theater, ceremonial dance, and feast
  • Mayor Masru arrived with four red-skinned oni guards—surprisingly unimposing, affable, and food-obsessed
  • Mido formally transferred power to Masru via the tea ceremony
  • A moth swarm destroyed five lanterns at the theater, alarming both Magistrate Kurosawa and Mido
  • Meilin the gravekeeper stunned the town with her transformation during the dance
  • A dwarven skeleton (Kaizo clan) was discovered in an old dry well
  • Liwen the florist revealed Kurosawa ordered five dozen white lilies (funeral flowers)
  • The party infiltrated the Yeshou estate via three routes: firefly through a window, koi pond tunnel, and shed tunnel
  • A fox construct alarm triggered in the magistrate's bedroom; Kurosawa rushed back to the estate
  • The Yeshou estate's koi pond passage is guarded by a mutated koi fish

docs.title: Session Five (2026-03-12)
docs.text:
• The party escaped the estate: Donkey as a firefly, Da Baishan through the front door with a bluff, Littlefinger through the shadows after stealing a military medal
  • Martial law declared by Mayor Masru and Magistrate Kurosawa—all citizens confined to homes, gatherings criminal, travel forbidden; decrees nailed across both sides of town
  • Oni guards actively patrolled both banks, escorting citizens home and confronting those outside
  • Donkey used Conceal Spell to cast Sleep on an oni guard enforcing the lockdown
  • The party was escorted back to Silver Mist Inn under guard
  • Mido revealed the existence of Gosembiki (五芒星, the Five Pointed Star)—haunted ruins 10–20 miles east of Willowshore, connected to the ley line convergence
  • Luda, a local seamstress and arcane supplier, reported suspicious ingredient purchases by Kurosawa
  • The town is now under active lockdown; the magistrate plans to investigate the estate break-in

docs.title: Session Six (2026-03-19)
docs.text:
• A shared dream spread through the town—multiple residents including the party, Mayor Masru, and Radiant Willow experienced vivid visions of the land crying out in pain, rivers running black, and ley lines warping under pressure
  • Kurosawa ransacked the party's rooms at Silver Mist Inn as part of his investigation
  • Mayor Masru hired the party as "Masru's Faithful Five" with daily stipends and investigative licenses, overriding Kurosawa's authority
  • The Yeshou family was sentenced to exile in five days by Kurosawa
  • Meilin now appears to serve as a personal attendant to the mayor at the estate
  • Captain Akoto identified as one of the oni guard captains patrolling the town
  • The party tested their credentials, questioned Akoto, visited the Yeshous, Radiant Willow, and the cemetery before leaving town east toward the Gosembiki ruins
  • One of Mido's guards was found transformed into an infected werewolf 8+ miles east of town and killed by the party

docs.title: Session Eight (2026-04-22)
docs.text:
• A chasm tore through town from the east, splitting farmland and buildings along a line parallel to the river—an aftershock of the party pulling Kurosawa's device from the Gosembiki ruins
  • Demons began climbing out of the chasm—three emerged on the first night; one was killed by the party, one by Kurosawa in a single nodachi swing, and one dove back into the rift
  • At least one commoner was killed in the street; livestock and river-fish were killed by the chasm's heat
  • Kurosawa invoked the Rite of Open Reckoning—an ancient Chu Ye legal procedure—against the party; Mayor Masru served as judge
  • Verdict: guilty of unlawful interference, reckless endangerment, and spiritual disruption. The party was disarmed and marched to a warehouse.
  • Radiant Willow was the only townsperson to raise her hand when Boone asked the crowd who had shared the dream
  • The Seven Colored Songbird Theater and the square around it served as the trial site

docs.title: Session Nine (2026-05-07)
docs.text:
• The party fled west along the riverside out of town immediately at session start; Kurosawa and two oni gave chase across the grasslands beyond the town edge
  • Kurosawa demonstrated open geomantic combat in the chase: an earthen-grasp that pinned Boone, a 5 ft × 10 ft earthen wall raised across the party's escape line. Kurosawa critically struck Boone twice, dropping him to 0 HP, and sliced Littlefinger near to death
  • Radiant Willow ran out of town toward the fight unarmed and pleading. Kurosawa hesitated, lowered his nodachi, and dropped the wall. The party surrendered out of respect for her
  • Marched back into town and held in a fishy warehouse near the river (~40 ft × 50 ft, no windows, double doors barred) under one oni guard at a time, four oni in shifts
  • The Yeshou family extracted the party at night through a floor-hatch into a smuggling tunnel under the warehouse — part of a much older network beneath Willowshore that the party had no idea existed
  • The hidden papers (including a damning planning note in Kurosawa's hand) were retrieved from the wall crevice north of town by Littlefinger, still where he hid them
  • Townspeople — Yong among them, who "shook his head, disappointed" — stood at street-edges and watched the party marched in, and stayed silent
  • After the escape the party crossed the river south of town and left Willowshore, headed for the Order of the Palatine Eye. The Yeshous remain inside, watching from hideouts

docs.title: Session Eleven (2026-06-04)
docs.text:
• The town did not appear directly, but the party learned the shape of the threat against it. The pool at the Hollow of Seven Cedars showed Kurosawa's Root rite already corrupting Willowshore's memory — neighbors forgetting kindnesses, Liwen not recognizing the party, Radiant Willow forgetting why she trusted them, Yong re-remembering the party as the cause of his son's danger. The corruption flows from the cedars into the town
  • The party's fight to save Willowshore is now explicitly a fight for the town's memory of itself, not just its safety — and the only known cure (killing the grove) carries its own heavy cost

docs.title: Session Twelve (2026-07-16)
docs.text:
• The Grandmother confirmed the corruption is real and already done: the town's good memories of the party are eaten and cannot be restored — "what went downhill must remain downhill." Reversing the rite will only stop further damage
  • The party returned and surveilled the town from the northwest tree line: life looked outwardly normal (the eastern chasm scar still visible), but the oni garrison has doubled to ~8 red-skinned guards, Kurosawa was nowhere to be seen, and a new blue-skinned oni administrator now issues orders from the Yeshou estate — obeyed even by Captain Akoto
  • Under cover of night, the party executed a clean heist of the estate — Donkey as a dragonfly through Kurosawa's office window, Littlefinger picking the lacquered chest's lock — stealing the Root Correction ritual, five copper nails, and Kurosawa's lockbox note. The fox construct arrived moments too late; no alarm was raised
  • The party withdrew west into the forest the same night; as far as they know, the town is unaware they were ever there

docs.title: Captain Akoto
docs.text: A soldier doing his job in a town that doesn't want him.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name Akoto Title Captain Ancestry Oni (red-skinned) Affiliation Magistrate Kurosawa's guard detachment Location Willowshore

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Captain Akoto is one of the red-skinned oni guards serving under Magistrate Kurosawa. He patrols the streets of Willowshore enforcing martial law. Unlike the magistrate, Akoto appears to be a straightforward soldier—he follows orders without relish and seems somewhat reasonable when presented with legitimate authority.

docs.title: Personality
docs.text: Akoto is professional and measured. When the party presented their investigative licenses from Mayor Masru, he was confused but compliant. He did not enjoy the confrontation and moved on without incident. He was willing to share basic information when asked directly, admitting that the troops were told very little about the investigation.

docs.title: Session Six (2026-03-19)
docs.text:
• Confronted the party on the street during martial law; ordered them back to their homes
  • Read the party's investigative licenses from Mayor Masru; looked confused but let them pass
  • When Ginkgo demanded his name and rank, he paused but complied: "Captain Akoto"
  • Admitted the troops were told very little by Kurosawa—only to enforce the lockdown and ensure no one leaves town
  • Confirmed Kurosawa was first on the scene at the estate and insisted on handling the investigation alone
  • Said he guessed the lockdown related to the commotion at the estate; expressed mild doubt: "I don't think Kurosawa will be as calm as I am"

docs.title: Session Eight (2026-04-22)
docs.text:
• Present during the demon attack on Willowshore; engaged one of the creatures alongside his fellow oni guards and townsfolk before the party arrived
  • Called as a prosecution witness during the Rite of Open Reckoning
  • Testified that he encountered the party leaving town toward the eastern ruins, and that they had stopped at the graveyard first "conversing in secret—possibly with the dead"
  • Did not mention that Donkey had put him to Sleep with Conceal Spell during martial law (either from omission, tact, or not knowing)
  • Concluded that the party "acted on their own authority, sought no counsel, and reported to no one"
  • Professional and measured in tone; offered no personal animosity, but delivered testimony that supported Kurosawa's framing of the party as unsanctioned operators

docs.title: Session Twelve (2026-07-16)
docs.text: • Observed from a distance during the party's surveillance of Willowshore: still on duty amid a garrison doubled to ~8 guards, and now following the orders of the new blue-skinned oni administrator operating from the estate in Kurosawa's absence

docs.title: Cassian Voss
docs.text: A scholar of forgotten gods, now a forgotten ghost.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name Cassian Voss Ancestry Human (presumed) Occupation Religious researcher / Scholar of "old gods" Origin Absalom (foreign continent) Status Deceased — spirit untethered Location Willowshore (spirit)

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Cassian Voss was a religious researcher from Absalom—a continent far from Tian Xia, making him one of the very few foreigners ever to pass through Willowshore. He arrived roughly two months before Magistrate Kurosawa, stayed for about a week, and then supposedly moved on to continue his research elsewhere.

In death, he appears as a translucent blue-white spirit—shimmering like heat haze off summer stone. Water drips from his face and the tattered sleeves of clothing that is distinctly not from Willowshore. He does not remember his own name.

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
Voss specialized in what he called "old gods"—elder deities that predated the current known pantheon across all traditions. His theory held that before the gods worshipped today, there existed a group of ancient, primordial entities—similar to how Greek mythology's Titans preceded the Olympians.

He came to Tian Xia specifically because its pantheon was unfamiliar to Absalom's scholars, and he hoped to find histories, oral traditions, or other links to these elder gods among the local religious practices. Very little is known about these entities, and Voss's work represented a rare scholarly effort to catalog them.

His research interests align disturbingly well with the dark summoning ritual later discovered at the Mourndusk Willow—a ritual that Mido identified as an invocation of the "Mother of a Thousand Wings", an ancient demon of pestilence.

Session Ten established that Voss carried the eye-mark of the Order of the Palatine Eye on the back of his hand — the same mark the cliff drew on Da Baishan. Session Eleven added the rest of the picture: Voss was at the Hollow of Seven Cedars, investigating the leyline that runs through it, six months to a year ago — months before Kurosawa drove his nails. He was, in other words, an Order investigator who found the grove before it was corrupted, and did not survive the finding.

docs.title: Death and Spirit
docs.text:
Voss was killed by a spirit at the river near Willowshore. Only Da Baishan and Ginkgo witnessed the death. Following Ginkgo's beliefs about the natural cycle, they hid the body in the forest rather than following local customs for spirit-death disposal.

Since then, Voss's spirit has appeared to Da Baishan and Ginkgo on multiple occasions:

  • During the graveyard combat (Session Two), the spirit appeared and shielded Da Baishan from the undead cat's attack
  • He spoke cryptic words: "You carry me into earth that is not my own. My ties bind beyond these roots. Something stolen, something broken."
  • He cannot cross to the afterlife until the mystery of his death is solved

docs.title: Personality (In Life)
docs.text: Based on Donkey's memories, Voss was a quiet, studious man who kept mostly to himself during his brief stay. He was noticeable primarily because foreigners from Absalom are exceedingly rare in this part of the world. He showed no signs of hostility or ill intent—just scholarly curiosity—and his departure seemed unremarkable at the time.

docs.title: The Carving at the Grove (Session Eleven)
docs.text:
On the third cedar of the Hollow of Seven Cedars, about 20 inches off the ground, Boone's "That's Odd" turned up a name that had been carved and then frantically scratched out with a blade — leaving only ·S·S·I·N·V·O·S·S. Da Baishan's recall pieced it into Cassian Voss. Around the gouges was a fine, mustard-khaki powder that Da Baishan identified (Occultism) as finely-ground moth-wing carapace — the same signature as the inauguration moths and the Mourndusk Willow wings.

Da Baishan's quick magic-identification placed the carving and powder at six months to a year old — faintly magical, older than Kurosawa's recent nail-and-graft work (which dates within a month of the party's arrival in Willowshore). Cross-referenced against the party's belief that Voss died ~6 months ago, the timeline lines up: Voss was at the grove, investigating its leyline, before Kurosawa got there, and his name was defaced around the time he died. The handwriting also looked familiar to Littlefinger — not Kurosawa's, but something he'd seen before and couldn't place.

This reframes Voss from a Willowshore curiosity into a predecessor: an Order investigator working the exact site that has now become Kurosawa's Root key, who got close enough to be killed and scrubbed from the tree.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• How did he actually die? — He was killed by a spirit at the river; the grove sits on that leyline. Was the grove (or its Warden, or the Dream Eater) what killed him — or did whatever Kurosawa later weaponized already stir there?
  • Who scratched out his name on the third cedar, and why? Kurosawa, to erase a predecessor? The grove itself? Voss, defacing his own mark?
  • Whose handwriting did Littlefinger half-recognize in the carving?
  • Is his death connected to the ritual? — He was Order, he was on the leyline, and he predates Kurosawa at the very site that is now the rite's keystone
  • What was "stolen" and "broken"? — His spirit's cryptic message suggests something was taken from him and something was violated or destroyed
  • Did someone else benefit from his death? — Could the ritual have required a sacrifice, and was Voss that sacrifice?

docs.title: Session Zero (2026-01-16)
docs.text:
• Da Baishan and Ginkgo witnessed his death by spirit at the river
  • His body was hidden in the forest against local custom

docs.title: Session Two (2026-02-05)
docs.text:
• His spirit appeared during the graveyard combat; shielded Da Baishan from the undead cat
  • Delivered cryptic message: "Something stolen, something broken"
  • Could not remember his own name or identity

docs.title: Session Three (2026-02-12)
docs.text:
• Identified by Donkey (Willowshore Lore 26) as Cassian Voss, a religious researcher from Absalom
  • His specialty in "old gods" / elder deities established
  • His timeline in Willowshore (arriving ~2 months before the magistrate) raises questions about the ritual's timeline

docs.title: Session Four (2026-02-26)
docs.text:
• Radiant Willow shared additional details from her conversations with Voss:
    • He described "five lines, five fortunes" converging at Willowshore—aligning with the ley line convergence Mido revealed
    • He said "something is being drawn here"—the only thing about his research that seemed to worry him
    • His last known plans were to head east, following the rivers where the ley lines tracked
    • He had originally wanted to go north into the Dark Woods, but Willow warned him against it
    • Willow described him as "an amazing researcher" who was "so excited" about his findings—she still believes he simply moved on

docs.title: Session Six (2026-03-19)
docs.text:
• His spirit was channeled through Da Baishan during a cemetery séance led by the party (Spirit Lore 18, Diplomacy check); spoke through Da Baishan's body while Da Baishan could hear but not control himself
  • The spirit was taxing and could answer only a few questions before fading:
    • On the old gods: "There were five. Despite the current understanding, they are much closer than the legends would like us to believe. It is not very difficult to commune with them." Also: "They are clever manipulators and to be seriously concerned."
    • On who benefits from bringing them back: "Foolish ones seeking power."
    • On the prophecy (artist, ruler, sage, lover, judge): "That is the ritual for the Unspeakable One. Five must be sacrificed to bring—" (cut off mid-sentence; the spirit was pulled away)
  • The Unspeakable One — a new entity identified by Voss's spirit as the target of a sacrifice ritual involving the five archetypes from the prophecy
  • His knowledge of the old gods continues to prove critical to the party's investigation even in death

docs.title: Session Ten (2026-05-14)
docs.text: • Recalled by Da Baishan at the Palatine Eye Vault: Voss carried the same eye-mark on the back of his hand that the cliff door drew — first hard evidence he was Order, or Order-marked

docs.title: Session Eleven (2026-06-04)
docs.text:
• His name was found carved and scratched out on the third cedar of the Hollow of Seven Cedars, packed with ground moth-wing powder — see The Carving at the Grove above
  • The carving dated 6 months to a year old, months before Kurosawa's corruption of the grove — establishing Voss as an Order investigator who reached the site first and died there
  • Littlefinger found the handwriting familiar but could not place it — explicitly not Kurosawa's

docs.title: Session Twelve (2026-07-16)
docs.text:
• The erasure of his name was confirmed to be a second, separate seal on the grove — see the two seals. Kurosawa's stolen lockbox note admits: "The name was taken before I came to the cedars. The powder is not mine." Even the magistrate does not know whose hand it was
  • The party's camp analysis characterized the erasure as done in "haste, emotion, emotional removal" — nothing like geomancy — in a script Donkey half-recognizes and cannot place
  • Ginkgo floated Voss himself as the second seal's author ("Who else would it be? Could it be Cassian Voss?"); Da Baishan: "He's all over this... I knew we should have buried him"
  • The Grandmother's reversal instructions — "give back the name and undo the seals" — may point directly at his scratched-out name

docs.title: Cliché
docs.text: "What could be more cliché than a blind man giving wise advice in the mountains?"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Name "Cliché" (self-given) Title / Role Mountain hermit; oracle on the road to the Order of the Palatine Eye Ancestry Halfling (apparent) — "about the size of a toddler, three and a half feet" Location A switchback landing in the mountains south of Willowshore, roughly halfway between Glutton's Bridge and the Palatine Eye Vault Status Walked off camp without anyone seeing him leave

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
A small, scrawny figure hunched beside a stone-ringed coal fire on a narrow mountain landing. Tattered Tibet-style fur wraps for warmth. An old straw hat. Cloth wraps wound entirely over the eyes. No one in the party has ever seen what is, or is not, behind them.

He poked the coals with a walking stick when the party crested the switchback. Round, dark, almost-perfect spheres of stone — he kept them in the fire and would, on some private signal, reach down for one and use it as a gift, or a punctuation mark.

When the party left, his stones were still in the fire pit. The fire was still warm. The hermit was gone, and there were no tracks.

docs.title: Personality
docs.text:
Cliché speaks in compressed, oblique riddle-talk — only ever as much as the listener can carry, never the literal answer. He never names anything by its proper name within earshot. The Order is "the eye." Kurosawa is "the smiling one" and Cheng Yesho is referenced obliquely. The PCs are not given their own names back; instead they are labeled by what he sees in them:
